title: 11 Herbal Creams For Menstrual Cramps
intro_desc: Herbal creams for menstrual cramps have become a popular alternative to traditional pain relievers. These creams typically contain a mixture of herbs that have natural anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties. One example is Zingiber officinale, or ginger, which has been used for centuries to soothe menstrual cramps. The active compound in ginger, gingerol, has been shown to reduce prostaglandins, hormone-like substances that cause the uterus to contract and lead to cramping.  Lavandula angustifolia, or lavender, is another herb often used in herbal creams for menstrual cramps. Lavender has a calming effect on the body and can help reduce anxiety and promote relaxation, which can help alleviate cramping. Its scent also has a natural pain-relieving effect, which can help reduce discomfort.  Valeriana officinalis, or valerian root, is a natural sedative that can help promote relaxation and reduce stress. It's often used in combination with other herbs to create a potent pain-relieving formula. When applied topically, valerian root can help reduce inflammation and soothe cramping.  Using herbal creams for menstrual cramps can bring several benefits to your life. For one, they can provide a natural and non-invasive way to manage pain. They also don't have the same side effects as traditional pain relievers, which can be a relief for those who experience stomach upset or other adverse effects. Additionally, herbal creams can be used in conjunction with other natural remedies, such as heat therapy or massage, to create a comprehensive pain management plan.  While herbal creams can be an effective way to manage menstrual cramps, it's essential to note that they may not work for everyone. Some people may experience varying levels of relief, and it's crucial to talk to a healthcare professional before trying any new remedies, especially if you have underlying medical conditions or take medications.

remedies.remedy_desc: Zingiber officinale creams contains active constituents like gingerol and shogaol, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. Gingerols and shogaols have anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties, making them effective in reducing pain and inflammation. These compounds work by blocking the production of prostaglandins, hormone-like substances that cause muscle contractions and pain during menstrual cramps. Additionally, gingerols and shogaols have a warming effect on the body, which can help to relax tense muscles and improve blood flow to the affected areas. By reducing inflammation and pain, Zingiber officinale creams can provide relief from menstrual cramps and improve overall comfort during this time.
remedies.image_alt: Zingiber officinale herbal creams for menstrual cramps
remedy_procedure: Mix 2 cups of coconut oil, 1 cup of shea butter, and 1/2 cup of beeswax in a small saucepan.
Heat the mixture over low heat, stirring until the beeswax melts.
Add 2 tablespoons of grated Zingiber officinale (ginger) root and 2 tablespoons of vitamin E oil to the mixture.
Remove the saucepan from the heat and let it cool for 10-15 minutes.
Strain the mixture into a clean bowl and let it cool completely before transferring it to an airtight container.

remedies.remedy_desc: Lavandula angustifolia creams contains linalool and linalyl acetate, two active constituents that help to ease menstrual cramps. These compounds have a relaxing effect on the body, reducing tension and promoting a good night's sleep, which is often disrupted by period pain. Linalool also has anti-inflammatory properties, which can help to reduce prostaglandins, hormone-like substances that cause the uterus to contract and lead to cramping. Additionally, linalyl acetate has a sedative effect, which can help to calm the mind and body, further reducing cramp symptoms. By reducing pain and promoting relaxation, Lavandula angustifolia creams can provide relief from menstrual cramps.
remedies.image_alt: Lavandula angustifolia herbal creams for menstrual cramps
remedy_procedure: Gather 1 cup of dried Lavandula angustifolia flowers and 1 cup of coconut oil in a heat-resistant bowl.
Heat the coconut oil in a double boiler or a saucepan with a bowl on top of a pot of boiling water, until it reaches 160°F (71°C).
Remove the coconut oil from the heat and add the dried Lavandula angustifolia flowers to the oil. Let it steep for 2-3 hours, or overnight.
Strain the oil through a cheesecloth or a coffee filter into a clean bowl, discarding the flowers. Add 2 tablespoons of beeswax and 2 tablespoons of shea butter to the oil.

Heat the mixture in the double boiler or saucepan over low heat, until the beeswax and shea butter are fully melted and the mixture reaches 120°F (49°C). Pour the mixture into a container and let it cool before use.

remedies.remedy_desc: Valeriana officinalis creams contains valerenic acid, valepotriates, and isovaleramide as its active constituents. These compounds have a sedative effect on the body, helping to relax the uterine muscles that contract during menstrual cramps. The valepotriates, in particular, have a spasmolytic property, which means they can help to ease muscle spasms and reduce pain. Additionally, valerenic acid has a calming effect on the nervous system, which can help to reduce anxiety and stress associated with menstrual cramps. By combining these properties, Valeriana officinalis creams can provide relief from menstrual cramps and promote a sense of well-being.
remedies.image_alt: Valeriana officinalis herbal creams for menstrual cramps
remedy_procedure: Gather ingredients: 2 cups of coconut oil, 1 cup of shea butter, 1/4 cup of beeswax, 2 tablespoons of dried Valeriana officinalis root powder, 2 tablespoons of vitamin E oil.
Melt coconut oil and shea butter in a double boiler or a microwave-safe bowl in 30-second increments, stirring between each heating.
Add beeswax to the melted mixture and stir until it's fully incorporated.
Add dried Valeriana officinalis root powder and vitamin E oil to the mixture. Stir well to combine.
Pour the mixture into a jar or tin and let it cool and solidify. Once set, your Valeriana officinalis cream is ready to use.

remedies.remedy_desc: Curcuma longa creams contains a compound called curcumin, which is a powerful anti-inflammatory agent. Curcumin has been shown to inhibit the production of prostaglandins, hormone-like substances that cause the muscles in the uterus to contract, leading to menstrual cramps. The antioxidants and flavonoids in Curcuma longa, such as demethoxycurcumin and bisdemethoxycurcumin, help to reduce inflammation and relax the uterine muscles. Curcuma longa creams may also contain other beneficial compounds like ferulic acid, which has anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. These properties work together to provide relief from menstrual cramps by reducing inflammation and relaxing the uterine muscles.
remedies.image_alt: Curcuma longa herbal creams for menstrual cramps
remedy_procedure: Gather 2 cups of coconut oil, 1 cup of shea butter, 1/4 cup of beeswax, and 2 tablespoons of turmeric powder in a bowl.
Melt the coconut oil, shea butter, and beeswax in a saucepan over low heat, stirring until smooth.
Add the turmeric powder to the melted mixture and stir well.
Remove the saucepan from heat and let it cool until the mixture thickens.
Pour the thickened mixture into a container, let it cool and solidify, then use as a cream for menstrual cramps.

remedies.remedy_desc: Glycyrrhiza glabra creams contains a compound called glycyrrhetic acid, which has anti-inflammatory properties that help to reduce menstrual cramps. Glycyrrhizin, another key component, has a natural pain-relieving effect that can ease discomfort. Licorice extract, a primary ingredient, also contains flavonoids that help to relax muscles and reduce spasms. The anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties of Glycyrrhiza glabra creams help to reduce prostaglandins, hormone-like substances that cause menstrual cramps. By reducing inflammation and muscle spasms, Glycyrrhiza glabra creams can provide relief from menstrual cramps.
remedies.image_alt: Glycyrrhiza glabra herbal creams for menstrual cramps
remedy_procedure: Gather ingredients: 2 cups of coconut oil, 1 cup of shea butter, 1/2 cup of beeswax, 2 tablespoons of glycyrrhiza glabra root powder, 10 drops of peppermint essential oil.
Melt coconut oil and shea butter in a double boiler or a heat-proof bowl over a pot of simmering water.
Add beeswax to the melted mixture and stir until it's fully incorporated.
Remove the mixture from the heat and stir in glycyrrhiza glabra root powder and peppermint essential oil.
Pour the mixture into small containers and let it cool and harden. Use as needed to relieve menstrual cramps.

remedies.remedy_desc: Cimicifuga racemosa creams contains a high concentration of sesquiterpenes and phenolic compounds, including actein and cimicifugoside. These bioactive constituents have anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce inflammation and ease menstrual cramp pain. Actein has been shown to inhibit the production of prostaglandins, hormone-like substances that cause the uterus to contract and lead to cramping. By reducing prostaglandin production, Cimicifuga racemosa creams can help relax the uterine muscles and alleviate menstrual cramp discomfort. The anti-inflammatory properties of cimicifugoside and other phenolic compounds also contribute to the relief of menstrual cramps by reducing inflammation in the uterine area.
remedies.image_alt: Cimicifuga racemosa herbal creams for menstrual cramps
remedy_procedure: Gather ingredients: 2 cups of dried Cimicifuga racemosa root, 1 cup of coconut oil, 1/4 cup of beeswax, 2 tablespoons of vitamin E oil, and 2 tablespoons of lavender oil.
Grind the dried Cimicifuga racemosa root into a fine powder using a coffee grinder or mortar and pestle.
Melt the coconut oil and beeswax in a double boiler or a heat-proof bowl set over a pot of simmering water.
Add the ground Cimicifuga racemosa root, vitamin E oil, and lavender oil to the melted coconut oil and beeswax mixture. Stir well to combine.
Pour the mixture into small jars or containers and let it cool and solidify. Store the Cimicifuga racemosa cream in a cool, dry place.

remedies.remedy_desc: Paeonia lactiflora creams contains active constituents such as paeonol, paeoniflorin, and benzenoid glycosides. These compounds have anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties, which help to reduce menstrual cramps by relaxing the uterine muscles. The antispasmodic effects of paeoniflorin and benzenoid glycosides also help to reduce smooth muscle spasms, further alleviating cramp symptoms. Additionally, paeonol has a sedative effect, which can promote relaxation and reduce pain perception. By addressing the root causes of menstrual cramps, Paeonia lactiflora creams can provide natural relief from this common issue.
remedies.image_alt: Paeonia lactiflora herbal creams for menstrual cramps
remedy_procedure: Gather 1 cup of dried Paeonia lactiflora flowers and 1 cup of carrier oil like coconut or olive oil.
Combine the dried Paeonia lactiflora flowers and carrier oil in a clean glass jar. Let it sit for 2-3 weeks, shaking the jar daily.
Strain the mixture through a cheesecloth or a coffee filter into another clean glass jar. Discard the solids.
Add 1 tablespoon of vitamin E oil to the liquid and mix well. Vitamin E oil helps to preserve the cream.
Transfer the cream to a small tin or jar and store it in the fridge. Use 1-2 teaspoons as needed to relieve menstrual cramps.

remedies.remedy_desc: Angelica archangelica creams contains angelic acid, ligustilide, and butylphthalide as key bioactive constituents. These compounds have anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties, which help to reduce menstrual cramps and alleviate discomfort. Angelic acid and ligustilide work by relaxing the uterine muscles and reducing prostaglandin production, a hormone that causes uterine contractions and pain. Butylphthalide has a sedative effect, helping to calm the nervous system and reduce stress and anxiety associated with menstrual cramps. By using Angelica archangelica creams, some women experience relief from menstrual cramps due to these specific properties and constituents.
remedies.image_alt: Angelica archangelica herbal creams for menstrual cramps
remedy_procedure: Gather 1/2 cup of dried Angelica archangelica root, 1/4 cup of coconut oil, and 1/4 cup of shea butter.
Boil 2 cups of water, then add the dried Angelica archangelica root and let it steep for 10 minutes.
Strain the liquid and let it cool. Mix the cooled liquid with the coconut oil and shea butter.
Heat the mixture in a double boiler or a heat-proof bowl set over a pot of simmering water, stirring occasionally.
Remove from heat, pour into a container, and let it cool before applying to the skin as needed for menstrual cramp relief.

remedies.remedy_desc: Foeniculum vulgare creams contains the active constituents anethole, fenchone, and limonene, which give it its medicinal properties. Anethole has a relaxing effect on the smooth muscles in the uterus, helping to reduce menstrual cramp pain. Fenchone has anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce swelling and ease discomfort. Limonene has a calming effect on the nervous system, which can help reduce anxiety and stress associated with menstrual cramps. By relaxing the muscles and reducing inflammation, Foeniculum vulgare creams may help provide relief from menstrual cramp pain.
remedies.image_alt: Foeniculum vulgare herbal creams for menstrual cramps
remedy_procedure: Gather ingredients: 1 cup of Foeniculum vulgare oil, 1/4 cup of coconut oil, 2 tablespoons of beeswax, 2 tablespoons of vitamin E oil.
Melt Foeniculum vulgare oil and coconut oil in a double boiler over low heat.
Add beeswax to the melted oil mixture and stir until fully incorporated.
Remove from heat and let cool slightly, then add vitamin E oil and stir well.
Pour the mixture into a container and let cool completely before use. Apply a small amount to the affected area for relief from menstrual cramps.

remedies.remedy_desc: Ginkgo biloba creams contains flavonoids and terpenoids as its active constituents. Flavonoids like quercetin and kaempferol have anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce menstrual cramp pain. Terpenoids like bilobalide have vasodilatory effects, which can help improve blood flow and reduce cramping. The antioxidant properties of ginkgo biloba's active constituents can also help reduce oxidative stress, a factor that may contribute to menstrual cramp pain. By reducing inflammation and improving blood flow, ginkgo biloba creams may provide relief from menstrual cramps.
remedies.image_alt: Ginkgo biloba herbal creams for menstrual cramps
remedy_procedure: Gather ingredients: 1 cup of coconut oil, 1/2 cup of shea butter, 2 tablespoons of beeswax, 2 tablespoons of Ginkgo biloba extract, and 10 drops of lavender essential oil.
Melt coconut oil and shea butter in a double boiler or a heat-proof bowl set over a pot of simmering water.
Add beeswax to the melted mixture and stir until it's fully incorporated.
Remove the mixture from heat and stir in Ginkgo biloba extract and lavender essential oil until well combined.
Pour the mixture into small containers and let it cool and solidify before use.

remedies.remedy_desc: Melissa officinalis creams contains bioactive constituents like linalool, linalyl acetate, and rosmarinic acid. These compounds have anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce swelling and pain in the uterus during menstrual cramps. The antispasmodic properties of Melissa officinalis also help relax the uterine muscles, easing cramping and discomfort. Additionally, Melissa officinalis has antispasmodic and anti-inflammatory effects that also help reduce pain and inflammation in the pelvic area. By applying Melissa officinalis creams to the skin, these bioactive compounds are absorbed, providing relief from menstrual cramps.
remedies.image_alt: Melissa officinalis herbal creams for menstrual cramps
remedy_procedure: Gather 1 cup of Melissa officinalis flowers and 1 cup of coconut oil in a saucepan.
Heat the coconut oil over low heat. Add the Melissa officinalis flowers and let it simmer for 2 hours.
Strain the mixture using a cheesecloth or a coffee filter into a clean bowl. Discard the flowers.
Add 1/4 cup of beeswax and 1/4 cup of shea butter to the bowl. Mix until melted.
Pour the mixture into small containers and let it cool. Use a few times a day to relieve menstrual cramps.

faq_prevent: Drinking herbal tea may help ease menstrual cramps. Some herbal teas, like ginger tea, have natural pain-relieving properties. Others, such as chamomile tea, can help calm the body and reduce discomfort. These teas may help you feel more comfortable during your period, but it's not a guaranteed solution for everyone.
faq_safe: Consuming herbal teas for menstrual cramps daily can be safe, but it depends on the type of tea and your body's sensitivity. Some teas, like chamomile and ginger, are commonly used and considered gentle. However, others might cause stomach upset or interact with medications. It's best to start with small amounts and observe how your body reacts.
faq_timeline: For some people, herbal teas like chamomile, ginger, and peppermint can start to ease menstrual cramps within a few hours. This is because these teas can help relax the muscles and reduce inflammation in the uterus. As you drink them regularly, you might notice a decrease in cramp severity and frequency over time, often within a week or two.
faq_time: Drinking herbal tea for menstrual cramps can be most effective when it's absorbed best by your body. This usually happens when you're most relaxed and calm. So, try sipping your herbal tea in the evening, before bed, when your body is winding down.
faq_interaction: Herbal teas can interact with prescription medications for menstrual cramps. For example, if you're taking blood thinners, certain herbal teas like ginkgo biloba and feverfew might increase the risk of bleeding. On the other hand, teas like ginger and raspberry leaf might interact with pain medications, affecting how well they work. Be cautious with your tea choices.
faq_pregnant: While pregnant, it's best to limit herbal teas, especially those for menstrual cramps. Some herbs might not be safe for the baby or could cause contractions. Peppermint and ginger teas are often okay, but it's still a good idea to check the ingredients and follow the recommended amounts.
faq_breastfeeding: Herbal teas for menstrual cramps can be a good option while breastfeeding, but be cautious with strong herbs. Some, like ginger and peppermint, are generally considered safe and can help with cramps. However, others like chamomile and raspberry leaf might stimulate milk production or interact with medications, so it's best to start with small amounts and see how your body reacts.
title_2: Say Goodbye to Menstrual Cramps with 11 Herbal Creams for Teens
